repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
My reasoning is: with module-state / freethreading, static storage in functions makes me increasingly unhappy. Neither of those apply to PyPy or GraalPy yet so it's more future-proofing. I also think the number of people using the Python 2 iteritems/iterkeys/itervalues is increasingly low, so I don't really care about caching a string for their speed.

Also refactor the string comparison a bit to try to skip the common chunks.

comment_data.body: Since we're only targeting Py3 these days, can't we translate the names at compile time?

Partly I think?

We don't necessarily *know* it's a `dict`. At least some of the time the function is called with things that we've guessed is a `dict` but might not be.

If it isn't a `dict` then we want to use the untranslated name - i.e. a user class might genuinely have `iteritems`.

We could add a `py3_name` argument that is translated at compile-time though, and the section could just be `name = py3_name`
Ok, let's *not* do that then. It never really bothered anyone that we're building a Python string here.
I think I'll do the translation in the cases where we know it's a `dict` because there's no reason not to.

I know, we're taking care of this elsewhere, but that's elsewhere.
```suggestion
            if dict_obj.type.is_pydict_type and method.startswith('iter'):
                assert method in ('iterkeys', 'itervalues', 'iteritems'), method
                method = EncodedString(method[4:])
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1131,6 +1131,8 @@ def _transform_dict_iteration(self, node, dict_obj, method, keys, values):
         body.stats[0:0] = [iter_next_node]
 
         if method:
+            if dict_obj.type.is_pydict_type and method.startswith('iter'):
+                method = EncodedString(method[4:])

comment_data.body:
If we want to keep the UTF-8 comparison, at least we shouldn't be getting here if the method name wasn't one of (iter)?(keys|values|items), so I think we can avoid the complete name comparisons at runtime.
```suggestion
            if (strncmp(name, "iter", 4) == 0) {
```
I've gone for checking the length since that's a bit more obvious to me than the 4th character.

Functionally questionable since `frozendict` never had `iter*` methods, but since users won't run into attribute lookup errors in CPython, failures in PyPy&friends might easily go unnoticed. Thus, leaving a functional difference between CPython and PyPy would be even worse.

OTOH, since starting to use `frozendict` requires code changes, wouldn't that be the right time to also replace all left-over `.iter*()` usages with the modern `dict` view based methods? I'd rather actively push users into updating their code *while they are changing it* than silently making `frozendict.iterkeys()` work.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1131,7 +1131,7 @@ def _transform_dict_iteration(self, node, dict_obj, method, keys, values):
         body.stats[0:0] = [iter_next_node]
 
         if method:
-            if dict_obj.type.is_pydict_type and method.startswith('iter'):
+            if dict_obj.type.is_pyanydict_type and method.startswith('iter'):

comment_data.body: This is implicitly assuming that the Limited API (and its users) will never enable `CYTHON_AVOID_BORROWED_REFS`, which I don't think is a safe bet. I guess we'll have to live with `__Pyx_PyUnicode_GET_LENGTH()` and its potential exception return here, much as I regularly bemoan it.

I agree it'd be good not to support `iter*` on `frozendict`. And also that PyPy and GraalPy should do the same as CPython.

The current situation (without this change) is that `frozendict.iteritems` works in Cython.  The change above makes it work a little faster, but doesn't alter any behaviour.

But we could definitely modify is so that `frozendict` doesn't work - that'd probably be better
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1131,7 +1131,7 @@ def _transform_dict_iteration(self, node, dict_obj, method, keys, values):
         body.stats[0:0] = [iter_next_node]
 
         if method:
-            if dict_obj.type.is_pydict_type and method.startswith('iter'):
+            if dict_obj.type.is_pyanydict_type and method.startswith('iter'):

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
static CYTHON_INLINE PyObject* __Pyx_dict_iterator_legacy(PyObject* dict, int is_dict, PyObject* method_name,
                                                          Py_ssize_t* p_orig_length, int* p_is_dict);
```

Spelling out both names makes them easier to grep for.
```suggestion
                    "__Pyx_dict_iterator_legacy" if legacy_method else "__Pyx_dict_iterator",
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1153,7 +1164,7 @@ def _transform_dict_iteration(self, node, dict_obj, method, keys, values):
                 lhs = dict_temp,
                 rhs = ExprNodes.PythonCapiCallNode(
                     dict_obj.pos,
-                    "__Pyx_dict_iterator",
+                    f"__Pyx_dict_iterator{'_legacy' if legacy_method else ''}",

commit.message:
Remove static PyObject* from PyPy/GraalPy __Pyx_dict_iterator

My reasoning is: with module-state / freethreading, static
storage in functions makes me increasingly unhappy. Neither
of those apply to PyPy or GraalPy yet so it's more future-proofing.
I also think the number of people using the Python 2
iteritems/iterkeys/itervalues is increasingly low, so I don't
really care about caching a string for their speed.

Also refactor the string comparison a bit to try to skip the
common chunks.
